summary: The entire stack MUST be deployable via docker compose up from a single repository.

The system MUST sustain 100 concurrent heavy users (sending 1 message per 10 s on average, streaming responses, mixed RAG/Graph) on a 16-core / 32 GB host without visible UI lag (p95 server processing ≤ 500 ms excludi...
All services MUST expose /healthz and /readyz HTTP probes for orchestration.

The system follows Domain-Driven Design with bounded contexts: Identity, Tenancy, Keys, Agents, Knowledge (RAG/GraphRAG), Conversation, Workflow, Audit, Notification. Each context owns its own modules/tables; cross-co...
Admin is platform-level and NOT assignable through the UI; set via a bootstrap CLI or a seed_admins env list on first boot.
The Original Creator flag on an Org is a separate, immutable bit, orthogonal to OrgOwner. The Original Creator is always an Org Owner and cannot demote, leave, or be removed.

Individuals can create projects without an Org; such projects are owned by the Individual (owner_type = 'user'). Org Owners are automatically Project Owners on every project in their Org (OrgOwner → ProjectOwner inher...
Guest is a registered Individual account that has been granted access to a specific Chat Room via an invite link. Guest status is per-Chat-Room; a Guest in Room A may be a full member elsewhere.
All authorization MUST be enforced server-side in a single permissions service. Frontend visibility is advisory only.

Sign-up: email + password + CAPTCHA challenge (hCaptcha or Cloudflare Turnstile — Admin-configurable). Password policy: ≥ 10 chars, ≥ 1 letter, ≥ 1 digit, ≥ 1 symbol. Hashing: Argon2id (memory 64 MiB, time 3, parallel...

Email verification: user receives a verification token link; account is in pending state until verified. Unverified accounts cannot create Orgs/Projects nor accept Guest invites. Verification tokens are delivered via ...
Login returns:
Failed-login throttling: 5 attempts / 15 min / account + 20 attempts / 15 min / IP → 15 min lockout, audit logged.
Password reset via emailed one-time token (valid 30 min, single-use).

Users can change their email (requires re-verification) and password (requires current password). A password change invalidates all existing refresh tokens of that user and denylists all currently-valid access tokens.

Account self-deletion soft-deletes the account; the Admin can still recover within 60 days. Exception: if the user is the Original Creator of any Org with other active members, self-deletion is blocked until the Origi...

Session termination: user can revoke individual refresh tokens ("sessions") from their profile page. Revoking a session also denylists the jti of any access token issued from that session's last refresh.
Org invitations: Org Owner sends invite by email. If invitee has no account, the invite link lands on a sign-up page; after sign-up + email verification, they are automatically enrolled in the Org.
Project invitations: same UX, Project-scoped.
Chat Room Guest links (§13): a URL token that anyone (registered or not) can open; if not logged in, user is redirected to register; after registration, they join the room as Guest.
Guest links cannot be revoked, expired, password-protected, or use-capped (explicit stakeholder decision, Q43). Guest access is revoked only by deleting the Chat Room or banning the specific user.
Bans (Admin only): can ban by user-id, by email, or by IP. Banned users/IPs receive HTTP 403 on any endpoint. All bans are audit-logged.
Supported providers and their declared capabilities:
Each provider has a validation endpoint hard-coded in the platform:

5.  Plaintext of the key is never returned to any caller, including the uploader, including the Admin, after the initial creation request ends. The API response after upload contains a masked preview (e.g., sk-ant-......
When a user leaves a Project or is removed, all key_projects rows where key.owner_user_id = removed_user_id are revoked. Active outbound calls using those keys complete but no new calls are issued.

Carried keys can be used by Project Owners to build Key Groups. Project Owners can view usage, can view rotation/limit settings, but cannot view the secret (R7.03) and cannot transfer the key to another project.
A Key Group is an ordered list of keys (explicit priority: 1, 2, 3, …). Stakeholder chose ordered priority over round-robin (Q27).
Rotation triggers are defined by the group creator (Q25). Configurable per group:
When all keys in the Group are unavailable:
Usage Limitation scope: set per Key, inside the Key Group. Unit: per hour (Q28). Fields:
The scheduler maintains a per-key sliding-window counter in Redis (1-minute buckets for the trailing 60 minutes) to enforce hourly limits accurately.
UI notification: when any key in a Group exceeds 80 % of *any* of its hourly limits, raise an in-app notification (Q29) to all Project Members with usage-view permission. No email or webhook.

Every outbound provider call is recorded: (key_id, agent_id, chatroom_id, input_tokens, output_tokens, request_ms, http_status, error_code_if_any, timestamp). This feeds the per-hour limiter and the UI usage dashboard.
Usage is retained for 13 months to allow year-over-year comparisons. After 13 months, rows older than the cutoff are aggregated into daily totals and the raw rows deleted.
SMAP does not reconcile with provider-side bills. The counters reflect only SMAP-side observations (stakeholder decision: no billing responsibility).
No API, UI, or admin tool shall decrypt a key to display it. Decryption is invoked only by the Agent Runner for outbound calls, and the plaintext does not cross process boundaries.
Any verified Individual can create an Org. The creator is tagged is_original_creator = true, role = OrgOwner. Both bits are persisted.
An Org may have multiple Org Owners.
The Original Creator cannot:
Only the Original Creator can delete an Org. Admin can also force-delete (platform governance).
Org creation automatically creates a default Project named Default Project (Q30).
Any verified Individual can create a Project they personally own (owner_type = 'user'), independent of any Org. They become Project Owner. They can invite other Individuals as Project Members.
Individual-owned projects cannot be moved into an Org (Q31: no migration).
Within an Org, all Org Owners are implicitly Project Owners of every project in that Org (computed at authorization time, not persisted, per R5.03).
Org Members are not implicitly Project Members; they must be invited to each Project individually.
Project Owners can invite/remove Project Members (both Org Owners and Org Members of the same Org, when Org-owned; any Individual when Individual-owned).
Deleting an Org or a Project soft-deletes it (deleted_at = now()). It becomes invisible to non-Admin users immediately.
A nightly job permanently deletes rows whose deleted_at is older than 60 days. Cascaded cleanups:
Within the 60-day window, Admin may restore (POST /api/admin/restore/{type}/{id}).

Individual account deletion triggers soft-delete of all Individual-owned Projects, and removes the user from all other memberships. For Orgs the user is the Original Creator of, see §8.5 — account deletion is blocked ...
The Original Creator can initiate a transfer to any other current Org Owner (not an Org Member; they must be promoted to Owner first). Targets who are not already Org Owners are rejected.
Transfer protocol:
At most one pending transfer per Org at any time. New requests while one is pending are rejected with 409.
When a user with Original Creator status attempts account self-deletion:

An Admin can force-transfer Original Creator bypassing consent, in cases where the Original Creator account is inaccessible (bounced email, permanent absence). Always audit-logged as org.original_creator_force_transfe...
A Project Owner may create unlimited agents. Hard platform cap: 1 000 agents per project (prevents runaway DoS).
Agents are not versioned; no export/import; no templates (Q41). Editing overwrites in place.
Deleting an Agent soft-deletes it for 60 days (consistent with R8.11) since agents may be referenced by historical chat messages.
prompt_strategy is per-agent and selectable by the Agent Designer.
When full: system_prompt is stored as a single markdown blob and sent verbatim.
When lazy: system_prompt is parsed as a multi-section markdown document. Each section must begin with a fenced YAML block:
Section bodies loaded within a turn are cached for that turn only; the next turn re-runs retrieval (agents are dynamic; authors can edit).
The lazy strategy is incompatible with providers that don't support tool use. For such a case, the system falls back to full silently and raises a UI warning.
context_mode = general: unbounded growth. The system sends the entire chat history (subject to provider's hard context limit, at which point the provider will error; this is surfaced to the UI).
context_mode = compact: when the running token count of the *next* request would exceed context_token_cap (default: 75 % of provider's context limit), the system runs /compact:
/compact is retryable; on failure the system keeps the original context and logs the failure to audit.
Transport: internal only, no external exposure. Messages flow via the event bus.
Message schema (JSON):
Each agent has an inbox (Redis Stream named a2a:agent:{agent_id}). Agents consume in FIFO order.
Synchronous A2A: the call type blocks the caller's current turn until a matching reply arrives (timeout default 60 s, configurable per call).
Asynchronous A2A: notify is fire-and-forget; instruct requires acknowledgement (see §15.6 for instruct semantics).
A2A access scope. An Agent may send A2A to another Agent only when:
Supported source formats: pdf, docx, md, txt (Q37).
Per-upload limit: 1 GB (shared with chat-room upload limit, §13). Files older than 3 days are auto-deleted from object store.
Parser pipeline:
Chunking strategy (Q37): user picks per RAG config:

Embedding model: choose per RAG config from openai:text-embedding-3-small, openai:text-embedding-3-large, gemini:text-embedding-004, voyage-3 (if user supplies Voyage key). Keys come from the owning project's Key Groups.
Vectors are stored in Qdrant with collection name rag_{project_id} and payload {doc_id, chunk_idx, agent_ids[]}.
Default top-k = 8. Configurable per agent.

Rerank (optional, Q37): cohere:rerank-3 or a local bge-reranker-v2-m3. User choice per RAG config. If Cohere, requires a user-supplied Cohere key; if local, uses the app's bundled reranker service (CPU inference accep...
Retrieved chunks are inserted as system-role messages tagged type:"rag" immediately before the user's turn.
Uploading RAG documents requires Project Owner role.
Documents are scoped per project; they can be shared with multiple Agents in the same Project but never across Projects.
Each Graph RAG config declares its own Key Group (Q40) — distinct from the consumer agent's Key Group, so graph-builder costs are billed to a separate owner if desired.

Build triggers (Q40) mirror the Agent wakeup_config, with one semantic difference: where an Agent's wake-up counts *user* messages, the Graph RAG builder counts all chat-room messages (user + agent). Supported triggers:
The builder worker consumes the *delta* since the last build:
Each build is transactional across Neo4j and Qdrant using a two-phase commit with compensation; a failure does not leave inconsistent state (see §11.2a).
A Graph RAG config is attached to at most one Agent (1:1 in v1).
At agent invocation time, the agent's turn triggers a hybrid query:
An agent declares a list of mcp_servers, each with {source, reference, allowlist_of_tools}.
Each MCP call is a distinct audit entry.
Every user-supplied MCP server runs in an ephemeral container with these constraints:
Egress Proxy is a FastAPI-based HTTPS forward proxy that:
Built-in code_exec uses the same sandbox but with a curated image (python:3.12-slim + common scientific libs) and the same gVisor policy.
URL-based MCP calls do not use the sandbox but are always routed through the Egress Proxy; the same allowlist applies.
The built-in web_search tool is a platform-written adapter with a pluggable provider interface. Supported providers in v1: Brave Search, Serper, Tavily, Google Programmable Search (CSE).

Search API keys are supplied by Project Owners, never by the platform. Keys are stored using the same envelope-encryption scheme as LLM provider keys (§7.6), in a separate row class (search_keys) so they cannot be con...
Per Project, at most one active search provider may be configured at any time; switching the provider is a single write that invalidates cache.
If a Project has no active search key:
Adapter contract. Each provider adapter implements:
top_k default 5, hard max 20. Results are capped at 4 KB total serialized size before being returned to the agent, to prevent context bloat.

Caching. The adapter caches (provider, query_normalized, top_k, locale, freshness) → results in Redis for 10 minutes (key search:{hash}) to avoid billing duplicate user questions. Cache is scoped per Project; no cross...
Rate limiting. Per Project, max 60 searches / minute (configurable by Admin) to shield against runaway agents. Exceeding the rate returns tool_rate_limited.
All search calls are audit-logged as mcp.tool_invoked with tool="web_search", truncated query preview (≤ 256 chars), provider name, HTTP status, result count.

Outbound search HTTP requests leave the platform through the Egress Proxy (§12.3), not from within any user-facing container. The Proxy's allowlist is seeded with the four providers' documented hostnames (api.search.b...

The v1 release ships one adapter implemented end-to-end plus the plug-in framework; the remaining three adapters are thin classes that conform to the same protocol and ship in the same release only after a parity test...
A Project contains N Workspaces, 1:N (Q42). Workspaces cannot be nested.
Every Workspace contains at least one Chat Room (Q42). Deleting the last Chat Room in a Workspace creates a new default one.
A Workspace can also define workflows (§15) that operate on its chat rooms.
These flags are independently togglable; any subset is valid. The UI prevents semantically useless combinations (e.g., project_owners_only = true while project_members = true is auto-corrected).
A Chat Room exposes a permanent URL of form https://<host>/g/<chatroom_id>/<opaque_token>.
Opening the URL without login lands on the registration page with the token preserved; after sign-up + email verification the user is auto-joined as Guest.
No expiry, no use cap, no password, no revocation (explicit stakeholder decision). To revoke access, delete the chat room or ban the user.
Supported input types: text (markdown source), image (png, jpg, webp), and document attachments (pdf, md, txt, docx). No audio (stakeholder removed voice support).
Single-upload size cap: 1 GB. Rejection at frontend and hard-enforced at gateway.
All attachments are stored in MinIO under /chat-uploads/{project_id}/{chatroom_id}/{msg_id}/{filename}. Lifecycle rule deletes objects after 3 days.
Messages keep a pointer to the object and, after expiry, surface the text [attachment expired] in the UI.
Supported rendering: full markdown, inline HTML (stakeholder explicitly allowed), fenced code blocks with syntax highlighting, LaTeX via KaTeX, Mermaid diagrams.

Image embedding via public links is allowed. There is no domain allowlist (stakeholder accepted the SSRF tradeoff). A warning banner explains that linked images are fetched by the user's browser, not by the server, so...

For HTML: double sanitization. On write, the backend sanitizes with bleach (Python) using the same allowlist as the frontend; the sanitized result is stored and indexed. On read, the frontend additionally sanitizes wi...
Messages are retained for 5 years from creation by default, platform-wide.
Users (with their permission scope) can manually delete messages; deleted messages are removed immediately from both DB and search index.
Users can export a chat room's history as JSON + a folder of attachments (attachments are regenerated from MinIO if still alive; missing ones are noted).
Search is database-only using PostgreSQL tsvector with GIN index on message content. No external search engine. Guest users can search only within rooms they belong to.
Clients connect to WSS /ws/chatroom/{id} after authenticating. Events pushed:
Server maintains per-room WebSocket hub. On backend restart, the client reconnects and requests a delta via REST (GET /api/chatrooms/{id}/messages?since=<id>).

Users may edit their own messages within 5 minutes of creation. Beyond 5 minutes the message is immutable to the author; only Admin/Project Owner can edit it. All edits preserve the original via a message_edits audit ...
Agents cannot edit their own past messages. An agent wishing to correct itself must send a new message.
Admin / Project Owner may edit any message in their scope; edits emit a message.updated WS event and an audit row message.edited_by_moderator.
Deletion (R13.16) removes the content row and index; edit history for that message is also purged.

Messages older than 5 years (R13.15) are hard-deleted nightly. Each purge emits an audit event message.purged_by_retention with {chatroom_id, count, oldest_kept_at}. Associated attachments (if still in MinIO) are like...

Stakeholder asked for enterprise-grade flexibility with a visual editor (Q48). The engine is a hybrid: nodes are placed on a DAG canvas, but each node is an FSM sub-unit that can self-loop and fire events. Concretely:...

A workflow has nodes of these types: trigger, agent_invocation, approval_gate, condition, instruct, subagent_spawn, wait_for_event, parallel, join, set_variable, end. The normative definition lives in docs/workflow.sc...
Workflow definitions are stored as JSON (workflow.definition_json). A separate JSON-schema validator ensures integrity.
Frontend uses Vue Flow (@vue-flow/core) for the canvas.
Each node type has a Vue component for its configuration panel (prompt, agent selection, branch conditions, timeout, etc.).

The editor supports: drag-and-drop, copy/paste, undo/redo, versionless save (edits are in place per R9.02), linting (unreachable nodes, missing references), and a dry-run simulator that steps through with synthetic in...
A workflow run is started by a trigger (manual, cron, message_received, a2a_event, or wakeup_signal — the full list matches trigger_config.trigger_type in docs/workflow.schema.json).
The engine maintains a workflow_run record with state: running | waiting | succeeded | failed | cancelled and a step_trace list of activity records.
Agent invocations inside a workflow respect all agent settings (wake-up, key group, prompt strategy). Workflow-issued invocations are logged with origin = 'workflow'.
The trace (Q55) is stored in the DB and visible to Admin + Project Owners in a dedicated backstage panel. It is not surfaced in the chat room UI.
every_n_messages.n counts all messages in the room (user + agent), scoped to the room (Q49).

silence_minutes.t_minutes starts counting when the room contains at least one connected live user and no message arrives for T minutes. Guests who are logged in but not inside the room do not prevent silence (Q49).
autostop_rounds: a "round" is an agent message followed by no user message (Q49). After autostop_rounds such rounds, the silence trigger stops for the room until a user sends a new message.
autostop_rounds default and hard cap: default 5, hard cap 100.
allow_self_open = false: an agent cannot speak first in a room where nobody has yet sent a message (Q49).
An Agent can modify only these two fields during a run, via a built-in tool update_wakeup({every_n_messages?, silence_minutes?}): every_n_messages.n and silence_minutes.t_minutes.
Hard bounds enforced server-side: n ∈ [1, 1000], t_minutes ∈ [1, 1440]. Out-of-range values are clamped and the clamp is audit-logged.
Platform Admin can also set *soft* per-agent bounds at creation time; self-modification must respect these.

The Agent Designer can configure a refresh_every_hours value. Every T hours, the wake-up configuration is reset to the Agent Designer's initial values (which equal the most recent human value since there is no version...
An Approval Gate node declares:
single: if the leader approves, passes; else fails.
majority: > 50 % of listed approvers must approve. Ties are broken by the leader.
consensus: all approvers must propose, debate, and converge on the same verdict. If not converged by timeout_seconds, the leader's verdict wins.
Approver agents consume tokens from their own Key Group (Q52: "whoever owns the key"). The leader agent's Key Group covers the final decision announcement.
An instructed agent cannot refuse. The instruction is enqueued into its A2A inbox with type = instruct.
Loop detection (Recommendation applied):
Instruction audit records include chain_id, path, issuer, target, payload_hash, result, chain_depth_at_issue.
An agent with workflow_capabilities.can_create_subagent = true may call spawn_subagent(parent_agent_id, task_description). The sub-agent inherits the parent's Key Group.
Recursion depth is exactly 1: sub-agents cannot spawn sub-sub-agents. Attempt raises an error and is audit-logged.
max_subagents_alive_simultaneously is configurable per parent agent (default 3, hard cap 20).

Life cycle: a sub-agent exists only for its task. On task end (completion or error), its runtime state and ephemeral context are purged. Sub-agents are persisted as ephemeral rows in agent_instances for audit purposes...
Sub-agent configuration inheritance (from parent agent):

Usage attribution for sub-agents. key_usage_events.agent_id stores the sub-agent's ephemeral id, and a new column parent_agent_id FK agents NULL aggregates cost to the parent for dashboard display. Billing (API provid...
View any Org, Project, Workspace, Chat Room, Key metadata, Agent configuration, Workflow run, Audit log, Usage report.
Ban/unban user by id or email; ban/unban IP range; the ban list is consulted by the rate-limit middleware.
Soft-delete any user; hard-delete (after 60-day grace) any user.
Impersonation for support: "view as" (read-only) per-user session, always audit-logged.
Admin never sees API key plaintext (R7.15) even via Admin tools.
Admin UI surfaces workflow traces, sub-agent chains, instruction chains, approval histories.
Retention: 365 days. After retention, rows are deleted nightly.
Visibility: Admin only (Q56). No other role sees audit logs.

Message-send events store metadata only: sender, room, byte size, hash of content; not the content (content is available in messages table). mcp.tool_invoked stores a truncated arg preview (≤ 1 KB) after secret redact...
Audit writes are append-only at the DB level via a trigger that denies UPDATE/DELETE except by a nightly retention job.
Channel: in-app UI only (Q29, Q58). No email, no webhook, no Slack in v1.
Events that trigger a notification:
Delivery: persisted in notifications table + pushed through the user's WebSocket presence channel (/ws/user/{id}). Bell badge reads unread count.

Every HTTP endpoint requires authentication (Q57). Two exceptions: /api/auth/register, /api/auth/login, /api/auth/request-password-reset, /api/auth/verify-email — all four are rate-limited aggressively.
Global middleware enforces (per-user-id AND per-IP) sliding-window rate limits stored in Redis:
WS connections: max 5 concurrent per user; excess connections are refused.
Admin can tune defaults via a rate_limit_policies table at runtime.
Banned users/IPs are short-circuited at the earliest middleware layer (403).

Rate-limit responses use HTTP 429 with headers Retry-After, X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset. Body is RFC 7807 problem+json with type = https://smap.local/problems/rate-limited.
Concurrency: sustain 100 heavy users, p95 end-to-end (excluding LLM roundtrip) ≤ 500 ms for API calls, ≤ 200 ms for WS event dispatch.
Horizontal scalability: FastAPI nodes are stateless; session/rate-limit state in Redis; scaling is linear by adding backend replicas behind Nginx.
Resource footprint (reference: single 16-core / 32 GB host): PG 4 GB, Redis 2 GB, Neo4j 4 GB, Qdrant 4 GB, MinIO 4 GB, Vault 1 GB, backend 8 GB, remainder headroom.
Startup: docker compose up brings the full stack to ready state in ≤ 3 minutes on a warm host.
i18n: all user-facing strings wrapped in translation helpers; English locale in v1; structural readiness for any number of locales (Q59).
Timezone: all timestamps in UTC at storage; the frontend renders in the user's browser timezone.
Browser support: last 2 stable versions of Chrome, Edge, Firefox, Safari. Mobile: iOS Safari 15+, Chrome Android 110+.
Accessibility: WCAG 2.1 AA target on core flows (login, chat, agent list).
Backup / DR: out of scope (Q61). The operator is expected to snapshot Postgres / Neo4j / Qdrant / MinIO volumes externally.
No public API (Q62).
No cross-context SQL joins. Cross-context reads go through the target context's application service.
Domain events are published via the shared event bus; handlers live inside the consuming context.
Every context exposes an application facade; api/ routers call only these facades, never repositories directly.
L0 presentational components (aka "dumb components") take props, emit events, have zero imports from L3/L4. They are portable to Storybook without mocks.
L2 API/WS is the ONLY layer that may import axios, fetch, or the native WebSocket constructor. Components, stores, and composables never touch transport directly.
Transport (the axios instance and WS client singletons) lives in shared/transport/ and is imported only by L2.
Views are thin: parse route params, call composables, pass props to components. No inline business logic.

Barrel rule. A slice's index.ts exports only what other slices may consume: route table, public composables, public types. Everything else (components, stores, queries, internal types) is internal. ESLint's no-restric...

Cross-slice dependency direction. Allowed: conversation → agents → keys → tenancy → identity → shared. Disallowed: any reverse direction. Codified in .eslintrc with boundaries plugin; violations fail CI.
shared/ has no imports from any slice. One-way dependency by construction.
TanStack Query owns every remote resource's cache. Query keys follow the pattern [slice, resource, ...params], e.g. ['agents','list', projectId].
Mutations use useMutation; on success they queryClient.invalidateQueries the relevant keys. WS events call the same invalidateQueries (or apply optimistic patches via setQueryData) — see §24.7.
Pinia stores NEVER cache server data. They hold session tokens, CSRF nonce (future), UI preferences, the view-as impersonation flag, and in-progress multi-step form drafts.

The JWT access token lives in a Pinia store kept only in memory. On page reload, the refresh token (kept as an httpOnly-like path via a secure refresh endpoint protected by SameSite=strict if cookies are ever added; i...
Axios interceptors (in order):

The OpenAPI-generated client (shared/api-client/) is the low-level layer: one function per endpoint, fully typed from the backend schema. Slice api/ folders wrap these into use-case-shaped calls (e.g., fetchAgentList(...

WsManager is a singleton. Slice composables acquire channels through wsManager.channel('/ws/chatroom/abc') which returns a typed Channel object exposing subscribe(eventName, handler) and send(payload). Reconnect, back...
A presentational component with a *Container.vue sibling is preferred over a single smart component, wherever the view is used more than once.
Naming convention: BaseFoo.vue (atom), Foo.vue (presentational), FooView.vue or FooContainer.vue (container). Storybook stories live next to presentational components in Foo.stories.ts.
Each slice exports routes.ts with an array of RouteRecordRaw. app/router.ts composes them and adds a single not-found catch-all.
Route meta declares auth requirements:
Guards are pure functions tested independently, composed in app/plugins/guards.ts:

Authorization on the frontend is advisory (per backend R5.05): every render-time permission check is paired with a server-side check. The frontend uses a <PermissionGate :cap="'create_agent'" :scope="projectId"> compo...
A slice subscribing to a WS channel exposes a composable, e.g. useChatroomSocket(chatroomId), returning reactive refs updated from server events. Internally, the composable:
No component subscribes to WS directly. Components read from query cache / Pinia; the composable is the sole WS-aware layer in each slice.
On reconnect, composables replay a delta fetch (e.g. GET /api/chatrooms/{id}/messages?since=) before resuming event application, to avoid gaps.
Every form uses vee-validate with Zod as the schema resolver. Schemas live in slices/<n>/types/ and are reused for API request validation (client-side pre-flight).
Backend RFC 7807 errors with detail.field_errors: [{path, message}] are piped to vee-validate's setErrors() so server-side validation appears as inline form errors without ad-hoc plumbing.
A <FormField> wrapper component handles label, error message, help text, and ARIA wiring — components never hand-roll this markup.
Base library: Element Plus (chosen over Naive UI for more mature mobile support and larger ecosystem). Imported on-demand via unplugin-auto-import to keep bundle size small.
Design tokens live in shared/styles/tokens.css as CSS custom properties (--color-bg-surface, --color-accent, --radius-md, …) and are themed light/dark by toggling a data-theme attribute on <html>.
No Tailwind in v1 — to avoid collision with Element Plus utility classes. Layouts use CSS Grid / Flex via scoped <style> blocks.
Scoped styles only. Global CSS is restricted to tokens.css, reset.css, and Element Plus overrides. A lint rule bans <style> (non-scoped) in any .vue file outside shared/styles/.
Breakpoints: 480, 768, 1024, 1280 px. Exposed as CSS variables and a useBreakpoint() composable so layout-sensitive components can react in JS too.
Chat: single-pane at < 768 px; side panels (agent list, attachments) become a drawer.
Workflow editor: < 1024 px shows a read-only canvas with a message to "switch to desktop to edit". Editing on mobile is out of scope for v1.
Touch targets are ≥ 44×44 px; tested via the dev's responsive toolbar + a Playwright mobile-viewport smoke suite.
Typed error classes in shared/errors/:
A global Vue errorCaptured handler routes:

console.error is redirected (in production) to a lightweight error beacon: POST to /api/csp-report for CSP violations and /api/frontend-errors (future) for uncaught JS. The latter endpoint is out of v1 scope; v1 just ...
A toast service (useToast()) is the one approved channel for user-visible transient messages. Components never use alert() or write ad-hoc banners.
No container component ships without at least one MSW-mocked integration test covering its success + one failure path.
E2E runs against docker compose -f compose.test.yml which stands up the full stack with a seeded fixture project.

Markdown → HTML pipeline in one file: slices/conversation/lib/renderMarkdown.ts. Uses markdown-it → DOMPurify (complementing backend bleach per R13.14). Outputs a v-html-compatible string. No other file in the repo ca...
KaTeX, Mermaid, and syntax-highlighting run after DOMPurify, using DOM-mutation APIs that cannot re-introduce script nodes.

Guest-link URLs contain the token in the path. The frontend strips tokens from Router history URLs after consumption (replace-state to /c/<chatroom_id>) so browsers don't leak tokens via Referer or history.
CSRF: N/A in v1 (token in Authorization header, not cookies). If cookies are added later, double-submit CSRF is mandatory; a placeholder composable useCsrfToken() is reserved.
Vite 5 with rollup output. One bundle per slice-entry-view lazy-loaded via dynamic import(); @vue-flow/core and mermaid and katex are isolated into their own chunks.
Locale bundles lazy-loaded on demand; en.json is the only bundle-at-startup locale.
Source maps emitted in production builds but served only to authenticated Admins via a private /admin/sourcemaps/ path (or not served; the map file is kept for post-mortem).
Bundle budget: initial page (login + root shell) ≤ 250 KB gzipped; per-view lazy chunk ≤ 200 KB. Violations fail CI (bundlesize).
tus endpoint: /api/tus (POST creation; HEAD/PATCH for resume).
Required tus headers: Tus-Resumable: 1.0.0, Upload-Length, Upload-Offset, Upload-Metadata.
Upload-Metadata MUST include filename, mime, kind, parent_id, parent_type.
Server-side enforcement: chunk size, total size, idle TTL on staged parts (24 h).
On completion, server returns the final resource location and persisted metadata.
Creation POST is rate-limited at 20/min/user; per-chunk PATCH calls at 300/min/user.
Virus scanning hook: post-completion file.scan_requested task; quarantine on failure with audit event.
